sentences.text: --Michael Jackson.
And we were starting with the-- we were continuing the story of Isaak Zummer.
Right This man, Isaak Zummer, I-- I call him.
I wrote his name as the [NON-ENGLISH]-- the angel that helps.
As he-- as I said, he-- first he said, you eat.
Sit down.
Eat.
And we told him that we-- we have-- my brother, Moshe has two-- a wife and two children that are in Istichka, in that widow's home.
He says-- he says, he knows where it is.
He knows where-- he said to us-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --he said, my brothers, he said, you must disappear tonight yet-- from here, because your life is in danger.
I have somebody.
I have a man that has a boat.
I am going over there.
And this man will-- will-- ferry you over the Dniester River tonight.
You must disappear tonight, yet.
Because if they come tomorrow, they send-- they will send-- send you to [NON-ENGLISH].
And that's-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and we said, how are you?
You are responsible?
How are you going to do it?
He said, you are six souls and I'm only one.
This man didn't have-- I didn't see a woman in this house.
He didn't have a beard.
He was walking [NON-ENGLISH] means without-- without a hat.
In other words, he was not religious.
But he was [NON-ENGLISH].
He said, you are a plurality.
You are a [NON-ENGLISH], and-- and I'm only one.
So don't worry about me.
Well, I'll cope with the situation.
And he left.
It took an hour or so.
And he came back.
And he told us that he has a man for us that will come-- 12 o'clock at night to the place in Istichka-- to that place.
And he will take us to the Dniester River and will ferry us over across the Dniester.
And he said that he-- when he ferries you over-- he told us-- when he ferries you over, you-- you have to go a few hundred steps up-- up the hill.
And then you make a left turn.
And then you hit-- you hit the highway.
And when you are on that highway, you go left until you see three white buildings.
The three white buildings is-- is-- a grain dealer.
One building is a grain storage, and the other one-- in one, is his house.
You will see a lantern burning on the table-- on it.
You knock at the door, and they will let you in.
And you will be on the other side of the Dniester River.
Now this was, again, against what we were supposed to do.
I mean, the Hungarians said they'll shoot us.
Anyway, we managed to get-- it was already dark-- we managed to get back on foot to that widow.
When we came, back we found our sister-in-law in hysterics, crying.
She got terribly scared.
We left in the morning for-- for a-- an hour or so.
And here we were detained the whole day.
They thought-- and-- and-- and-- and it was-- it was terrible.
And then we told-- we told her what-- what-- happened and what is going to-- to-- to be.
This Isaak Zummer told us also, he said, you are going to be over on the other side in this man's house.
And-- and-- and for-- for-- I regret very much that I don't remember their names-- the-- the grain dealer's name.
From there, they will-- they will get you to a city called Horodenka.
And in that city, there is a Judenrat there.
And there are thousands of Jews live in that city.
And they are taking-- taking care of-- of Hungarian refugees.
And he said-- this was four days before Rosh Hashanah.
Rosh Hashanah usually starts September.
And don't forget, we-- we left August the 3rd.
And-- and the reason why I remember that, I-- I'll-- I'll skip it now.
I remember it, because we want to go-- get ahead a little bit.
He said, before Rosh Hashanah, I will come to see you-- to see you on the other side of the Dniester River, yet.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I just want to mention now, when I came to the United States, first thing I did was I put in an ad in the-- in the [NON-ENGLISH] journal, the-- the-- the daily newspaper that appeared in New York City and under forward-- asking people if they know the man, Isaak Zummer, from that and that area-- if he survived.
Sadly, I never got any answer or anybody that knows.
I'm not finished with him.
Exactly we told the-- the-- the-- people that we stayed-- that widow-- and her daughter and son-in-law-- we told them what happened to us.
And we told them that Isaak Zummer hired somebody.
And did-- she-- they assured us if Isaak Zummer did, then you rest assure, he has-- he has reliable people.
Exactly around 12 o'clock at night, the man came.
And the-- the week before Rosh Hashanah, it is customary for Jews, religious Jews, to say [NON-ENGLISH].
Did you hear the word, [NON-ENGLISH]?
Where you get up early, be early into the synagogue, say [NON-ENGLISH].
In this widow's house, they had one room, so the-- and there was one bed in the room.
So my sister-in-law and the children slept in that bed.
And the three brothers, the three of us, slept on the floor.
And I remember we were-- we were laying on the floor.
And my brother, Moshe-- --he was-- he was the-- the pious, the religious one in our family.
And-- and maybe the-- the-- the weight of the responsibility that he had there-- his two little children and his wife-- he-- I remember, took out a [NON-ENGLISH].
He didn't go to sleep till 12.
He took out a [NON-ENGLISH].
And he asked us, he asked my brother-- Tsvi and I-- he says, what day of [NON-ENGLISH] is there?
We knew it's before Rosh Hashanah.
What day of [NON-ENGLISH] is it?
And we figured it out somehow that this is the fourth day of [NON-ENGLISH].
That is said before Rosh Hashanah.
That's why I knew it was before Rosh Hashanah, because we didn't say [NON-ENGLISH].
I remember-- I remember watching him.
And I remember seeing-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tears coming down his eyes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING] Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] exactly 12 o'clock, we said goodbye to the people of-- we didn't want to wake them up.
We said goodbye by then.
They-- they did come out and-- to say goodbye to us.
And the man came.
And we took-- and we were afraid that we will wake the children up.
And the children will start crying.

Up to this day, I wonder what kind of miracles that the children didn't-- didn't-- they-- they-- they-- there's some kind of a mysterious force in them that kept them-- that they knew what-- what the situation is.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I remember I was carrying the little [NON-ENGLISH] in my hand.
And Chani was-- was walking already with her mother.
And my brother, Tsvi and I, we went-- we had to walk a few kilometers, maybe two kilometers-- and-- to the Dniester River, to where he had anchored his-- his-- his boat.
And when we got closer, really, the roar, the water, the roar was-- was tremendous.
This is a wild, ferocious river.
And when we got there, when I took a look at this little boat, I thought, oh my God.
Here we are, six people-- the guy-- seven.
And-- and-- and this whole boat is-- it's-- is-- is-- is the-- the size of a big-- big--
If we would have seen that boat, that so-called boat, we would have never gotten into it.
There was no way back.
We got into that boat.
And the guy started navigating that boat, and-- and cutting.
The-- the-- the stream was so strong that it pulled us down, down, down.
Anyway, I don't know.
Maybe it didn't take more than 10 minutes.
Maybe-- maybe it didn't take more than 10 minutes, but it was an eternity till we-- we finally got across the Dniester River-- across.
And the way they-- they told us, exactly that's the way we went.
He-- he right away turned around and-- and-- and got us-- went back.
And we-- it was pitch dark-- pitch dark.
We couldn't-- but once we got on the highway, fortunately, we didn't see a soul.
We walked.
There was only a half a kilometer, he told us.
And then on the right side, all of a sudden, the-- the-- the three white buildings appeared.
I started running-- to that-- to-- to the building when-- when-- as-- when as the building came in-- in-- in view.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] when I got to the-- to the door, the door of this building, and I saw there was a lantern on the table.
But I rapped at the door.
But I was still not sure which one it is.
But I started-- I had a-- a-- a-- with my hand, reach out to see if there's a mezuzah on there.
And I-- and I-- and I saw it was there.
That was a great relief to know that you found the right place.
And then a lady opened up a window.
The window, she opened up.
And she said-- she said in a-- in a Polish Yiddish, [NON-ENGLISH]?
Yes, [NON-ENGLISH].
How many?
She asked, how many are you here?
So we told her.
She opened the door and let us in.
Meantime, there was-- there were already people sleeping on the-- on the floor there.
And they gave us a room.
We got into the room.
There was a bed.
And we-- we men, we-- we-- by that time, we were already plenty filthy.
And we didn't even want to get into a bed.
They offered us something to eat.
But we didn't-- this excitement and this fear for the Dniester to go over against what we were told-- we are going to be-- killed if they find us on this side of the Dniester.
So anyway, we-- we stayed there overnight.
I think we stayed there two days.
And the situation was this.
This-- this-- grain dealer's son-in-law was a physician.
And he worked in the city of Horodenka.
The city of Horodenka was a few miles away.
And he had a permission from the Germans-- for the German authorities-- that he was-- he was riding a motorbike back and forth home.
That was his home where his father-in-law was there.
And he was the liaison between the Jewish community center, the Judenrat, in Horodenka.
And they-- he-- they-- knew how many people managed to cross-- to come across the Dniester River.
And Horodenka had already, a Judenrat, they had kitchen.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But the Jews over there at that time already were ordered to-- to wear white bands with the-- with the [NON-ENGLISH] with bands.
So this-- this doctor's wife, she-- she gave us white-- white bands.
And she ferried us over, one by one, to the city of Horodenka.
The Judenrat evidently told her to take us to a place, to a Jewish home, that-- the name of the family was Berl Gefner.
It's another family's name that I can never forget.
We came in in there.
It was a-- a huge house.
But the-- the poverty of-- was-- was screaming from-- from all corners.
There was an old couple-- husband and wife, and no-- no children there.
And-- but anyway, we-- we got a roof over our head.
We got in-- in there.
Then we found out that in Horodenka was-- that was a-- a-- a hub for Hungarian soldiers.
And if you had some Hungarian money, everybody would take it.
You could buy-- you could buy some-- some food.
So we stayed in this-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] oh, wait.
This was the fourth day before Rosh Hashanah.
And we got the next day to this Berl Gefner in to Horodenka.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And a day or two later, that Mr.
Isaak Zummer came across, and evidently, he found out exactly where and what place we are.
He came to see us.
And he brought us bread.
But more than that, he-- he brought us a half a pound of tobacco-- that was cut up.
Tobacco was-- was like-- like gold-- gold standard during the wartime.
It was a shortage.
He said, you hold onto this, because you are going to go-- the closer you are going to go back to the border, the-- the-- the-- the-- the hunger over there is terrible.
Keep this here for tobacco.
You will be able to-- to-- to buy-- to buy some food.
And then he said, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you come with me.
So my brother, Tsvi and I went.
We left my brother, Moshe.
And this was a day or two before Rosh Hashanah, 1941.
The Jewish calendar would be 9th Rosh Hashanah, [NON-ENGLISH], 1942.
I mean, [NON-ENGLISH] is-- is-- we-- we-- he said, I have some people, farmers here.
I'll take you over there.
And I went with them-- with him.
And he came into our house.
And he started asking-- he knew the children.
What is this?
What does this boy do?
And what is this daughter doing?
And he said to the-- to the owner of that farm there, he said, I have here these people.
Here you give them whatever you have in flour.
You know, give them whatever you have finished-- flour-- and I'll-- I'll give you double back.
Just give them.
So they loaded-- this woman in the house-- they loaded us up with 10 pounds of flour, or more, and eggs, and cheese.
And I remember tomatoes, and apples, and-- and kraut, and bread.
Whatever baked bread they had, they gave us.
So that-- and this was a day-- a day or two before Rosh Hashanah.
So when we came back-- this is that Isaak Zummer-- he knew them.
He knew.
When they came back-- when we came back, we had already-- we were able to have bread for-- to bake bread for-- for-- for the holiday of Rosh Hashanah.
How much time do we have?
Six minutes.
All right.
So my-- my sister-in-law, she-- she took to-- to-- to bake-- bake some-- some challah baked bread.
And we had apples.
And we had-- we made-- we prepared a meal for Rosh Hashanah.
Rosh Hashanah we saw-- nobody.
This-- the-- the-- the man from the house, he said, he isn't going into.
There is no-- no-- shul or anything.
There isn't going out.
He'll stay in the house.

But when I looked out the window of the house, I saw catty corner the-- the-- third or fourth house down the street in the city, that there were people with white bands walking-- a lot of people into this-- this house.
So I-- I talked to this man, you know, from the house, Berl Gefner.
He said they-- they probably have a minion there.
They have a minion there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
We didn't go out.
We stayed all in this room-- Rosh Hashanah.
Then suddenly, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Manci and the children were in bed.
And we were also laying-- laying down-- kind of-- what is there to do?
We had one [NON-ENGLISH] that Moshe would look into it.
My brother, Tsvi and I wouldn't even look into the [NON-ENGLISH] of the Rosh Hashanah.
And suddenly, I see a truck stops in front of the house-- four soldiers-- German soldiers get off, and an officer.
And he comes into the-- to this room in our-- he opens up, comes in.
And he sees everybody is laying down.
And the German officer says, [NON-ENGLISH]?
He says, what the hell is this?
Is this a hospital?
Everybody's laying around.
I don't know.
I don't remember.
I don't remember who answered him.
Somebody answered him.
It's-- today is a Jewish holiday.
That's why we are here.
Then I overheard that he talked to the two soldiers.
They were eyeing the furniture in the house.
But it was very poor-- very poor furniture.
He says, [GERMAN].
And they-- they walked out of the house.
Then I watched through the window.
And I saw that they were walking right across the street to the other-- to another house.
And a little while later I started seeing-- they started moving out sofas, and chairs, and things-- they were emptying out.
Those furniture that was in Berl Gefner's house, they didn't-- it didn't suit him.
That was a army Juden.
So that was our greeting for the year [NON-ENGLISH].
